comment: This paper has been accepted for publication at the IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS), Pittsburgh, USA, 2026. 8 pages, 8 figures
summary: Reinforcement Learning has revolutionized the landscape of robotic research, allowing robust learning of complex robotic skills in simulation. However, real-world deployment in open-ended environments requires strong safety guarantees to prevent dangerous or harmful behaviors. Safe Reinforcement Learning methods address this requirement by enforcing safety constraints. Nevertheless, learning under constraints often reduces learning speed and could lead to suboptimal task performance, as the agent must solve a more complex constrained optimization problem compared to unconstrained settings. To tackle this issue, in this work, we propose an extension of the ATACOM framework, a state-of-the-art reliable safety layer that can be integrated with existing Reinforcement Learning algorithms to enforce constraints derived from prior knowledge of the system or learned directly from data. Our proposed method, named ATACOM Directional Constraints (ATACOM-DC), significantly improves the safety-performance trade-off by introducing directional constraints that distinguish between actions approaching and moving away from constraint boundaries, activating constraint enforcement only when necessary. We evaluate our method across a range of challenging robotic control tasks in simulation, analyzing both constraint-violation costs and achieved task performance. Code and additional material at https://atacom-dc.robot-learning.net.

AI.tldr: This paper presents ATACOM Directional Constraints (ATACOM-DC), a method to improve the safety-performance trade-off in Safe Reinforcement Learning by selectively applying safety constraints.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement Learning achieves high performance in simulations, but real-world deployment requires enhanced safety measures to avoid harmful behaviors. Existing Safe Reinforcement Learning approaches often slow down learning and degrade performance, necessitating improved methods.
AI.method: The authors extend the ATACOM framework by introducing directional constraints that enforce safety measures only when an action moves toward constraint boundaries. This selective application of constraints minimizes unnecessary enforcement.
AI.result: ATACOM-DC achieves a better balance between safety and task efficiency. Evaluations on various robotic control tasks demonstrate reduced constraint violations and high task performance.
AI.conclusion: ATACOM-DC effectively addresses safety concerns in Reinforcement Learning while maintaining robust task performance, making it a significant advancement for real-world robotic applications.

title: Sparse Inter-Layer Dependencies of Transformer FFN Neurons
summary: Feedforward network (FFN) blocks account for a large fraction of the parameters and computation in Transformer architectures, yet their internal structure remains difficult to interpret due to the additive superposition induced by the residual stream. We examine whether the activation of an FFN neuron can be explained by a sparse set of preceding neuron activations and attention outputs. We introduce a training-free attribution method that estimates the relative influence of upstream neurons and attention outputs on a target neuron's activation. Empirically, across models and layers, we find that small subsets of preceding activations and attention outputs suffice to preserve neuron activations with high fidelity when all remaining inputs are masked with their average values. Effective sparsity is even greater when accounting for the inherent activation sparsity of upstream layers. Moreover, applying the neuron-specific masks in all layers simultaneously, such that the induced deviations propagate through the network, leaves model perplexity largely unchanged at moderate sparsity levels. These results demonstrate that, despite dense parameterization, FFNs exhibit sparse and structured inter-layer dependencies at the neuron level. Our method provides a practical, scalable tool for circuit-level interpretability and identifies candidate sparse pathways with potential implications for efficient inference.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates feedforward network blocks in Transformers, uncovering sparsity in their neuron-level dependencies and introducing a method for understanding their internal structure.
AI.motivation: FFN blocks in Transformers are computationally and parameter heavy, yet their internal workings remain challenging to interpret.
AI.method: The authors propose a training-free attribution method to estimate the relative influence of upstream neurons and attention outputs on target FFN neuron activations.
AI.result: Small subsets of preceding activations and attention outputs can maintain neuron activations with high fidelity, and applying neuron-specific masks globally results in minimal impact on model performance at moderate sparsity.
AI.conclusion: FFNs display sparse and structured inter-layer connections at the neuron level, suggesting potential for efficient and interpretable Transformer designs.

title: Function-Aware Fill-in-the-Middle as Mid-Training for Coding Agent Foundation Models
summary: Coding agents must integrate external tool returns into ongoing reasoning - a capability that standard left-to-right pretraining on code exposes only in its forward direction. We observe that the action-observation-continuation loop of a coding agent is structurally isomorphic to a function call site, where a caller binds arguments, a callee returns a value computed elsewhere, and downstream code consumes that value. This conditioning structure exists at internet scale in ordinary code. We exploit it through function-aware fill-in-the-middle (FIM) mid-training: a self-supervised objective that masks functions selected via program dependency graph analysis and a complexity-inferability double criterion. We mid-train Qwen2.5-Coder-Instruct (7B/14B) and Qwen3-8B on a 2.6B-token decontaminated corpus drawn from 968 GitHub repositories, then apply existing agentic post-training pipelines. Mid-training improves SWE-Bench-Verified by +2.8/+3.0 at 7B/14B and by +3.2 on Qwen3-8B; SWE-Bench-Lite gains are +3.7/+4.0/+5.4 on the same models. The improvement holds across two post-training pipelines (R2E-Gym, SWE-Smith) and on a non-Qwen2.5 base (Qwen3-8B with SWE-Lego). Beyond in-domain gains, mid-training also mitigates the capability erosion that agentic post-training otherwise inflicts on non-agent coding (e.g., LiveCodeBench) and non-coding tool-use benchmarks (tau-bench, BFCL): although the mid-training corpus contains Python code only, the function-call inductive bias survives post-training and yields consistent gains.

AI.tldr: The study introduces function-aware mid-training to enhance coding agents' ability to integrate external tool returns in reasoning, achieving performance gains across benchmarks and preserving capabilities in agentic post-training.
AI.motivation: Standard pretraining fails to effectively leverage function call structures in coding agents, which are critical for integrating external tool feedback during reasoning.
AI.method: Developed function-aware fill-in-the-middle (FIM) mid-training using a self-supervised masking objective based on program dependency graph analysis and complexity-inferability criteria, applied to large-scale Python dataset.
AI.result: Models trained with FIM mid-training showed significant improvements across coding benchmarks at various model sizes and retained non-coding capabilities despite agentic post-training, demonstrating robustness of the function-call inductive bias.
AI.conclusion: Function-aware mid-training is effective in enhancing coding agents both in domain-specific tasks and broader tool-use benchmarks, offering a robust solution to mitigate capability erosion in agentic post-training.

title: QUBO-Optimized Evidence Selection for Retrieval-Augmented Question Answering with Unconventional Solvers
summary: Retrieval-augmented question answering depends on selecting evidence passages that jointly support answer generation. However, many RAG pipelines rely on top-\(k\) ranking, where passages are selected mainly by individual relevance scores, even though multi-hop questions often require complementary evidence satisfying multiple information requirements. Recent LLM-based selectors address this by treating retrieval as set selection, but using an LLM for this intermediate stage can be costly and difficult to scale. In this work, we formulate evidence selection as a Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO) problem. Given a question, candidate passages, and decomposed information requirements, our method constructs an energy function that balances relevance, requirement coverage, support strength, redundancy, complementarity, and compactness. Low-energy solutions correspond to compact evidence subsets that cover the needed requirements while avoiding unnecessary or repetitive context. The selected passages are then passed to a downstream language model for answer generation, separating combinatorial evidence selection from semantic answer generation. We evaluate the proposed QUBO selector on HotpotQA and compare it with LLM-based set selectors and non-LLM baselines including BM25, relevance top-\(k\), maximal marginal relevance, hybrid lexical--semantic ranking, greedy coverage, and random selection. The QUBO selector achieves competitive exact-match and token-F1 performance relative to LLM-based selectors while providing a solver-compatible formulation for structured evidence selection. These results suggest that multi-hop evidence selection can be cast as discrete optimization, opening a path toward RAG pipelines where LLMs are reserved for semantic processing and answer generation, while context selection is handled by Ising/QUBO-compatible solvers.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO) method for multi-hop evidence selection in retrieval-augmented question answering, aiming to improve downstream answer generation without relying heavily on LLMs for intermediate steps.
AI.motivation: To address the shortcomings of current retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipelines, particularly in multi-hop question answering, where selecting complementary evidence to meet multiple information needs is a challenge. Specifically, existing methods overly depend on individual relevance ranking or costly LLM-based selectors.
AI.method: The authors propose formulating evidence selection as a QUBO problem. This approach constructs an energy function to optimize relevance, requirement coverage, support strength, redundancy, and compactness. Low-energy solutions represent optimized subsets of evidence for a given question, which are then passed to a language model for answer generation.
AI.result: The proposed QUBO selector was tested on the HotpotQA dataset and compared against traditional and state-of-the-art baselines, including both LLM-based and non-LLM-based selectors. It achieves competitive exact-match and token-F1 scores, demonstrating its feasibility as an alternative evidence selection approach.
AI.conclusion: Formulating multi-hop evidence selection as a QUBO problem is both efficient and scalable. By separating evidence selection from semantic answer generation, LLMs can be reserved for their strengths, such as high-level reasoning, enabling more resource-efficient RAG pipelines.

title: Software Supply Chains are Dead: Use-Case-Oriented Regeneration
comment: [ESEM'26-ERVR] Proceedings of the 20th International Symposium on Empirical Software Engineering and Measurement (ESEM 2026 Emerging Results, Vision, and Reflection Papers)
summary: Modern software development relies on an increasingly doubtful premise: that the up-front implementation savings from adopting a dependency outweighs the maintenance costs. Two changes are reshaping the build-vs.-reuse calculus: software supply chain attacks have raised the cost of external reliance, while generative AI has lowered the cost of local implementation. We envision use-case-oriented regeneration as a new software sourcing paradigm that shifts the supply chain from external trust to local verification. We evaluate an agentic workflow that synthesizes only the specific slice of dependency functionality that a repository exercises. Our measurements across 180 repository-dependency pairs suggest that this approach is feasible: the replacements preserve 99.8% of repository-observed behavior across baseline validation checks and reduce the exported API surface by 93%. Software sourcing may evolve toward verifiable repository-specific code synthesis, especially when the required functionality is narrow, stable, and well tested.

AI.tldr: The abstract presents use-case-oriented regeneration as an alternative to traditional software dependency adoption, focusing on synthesizing functionality locally. This is particularly relevant as supply chain attacks increase and generative AI reduces implementation burdens.
AI.motivation: The current reliance on third-party dependencies in software development is becoming riskier due to rising software supply chain attacks. At the same time, generative AI technologies are lowering the cost of creating locally implemented software solutions. These trends create a need for new strategies for balancing build vs. reuse decisions.
AI.method: The authors propose and evaluate an agentic workflow that generates specific, repository-tailored slices of dependency functionality using local synthesis. They measured performance across 180 repository-dependency pairs.
AI.result: The study demonstrated that this approach preserved 99.8% of functionality observed in repository checks while reducing the API surface by 93%, illustrating strong feasibility of the method.
AI.conclusion: The findings suggest a future shift toward repository-specific, verifiable code synthesis, especially for use cases that require narrowly defined, stable, and well-tested functionality, reducing reliance on external dependencies.

title: GeoSEAN: Explainable Country-Level Image Geolocation for ASEAN Regions
summary: Image geolocation aims to infer the geographic origin of an image from visual content alone. However, this task remains challenging in regions where countries share similar urban, roadside, architectural, and environmental characteristics. Many existing geolocation models focus on coordinate level prediction or classification performance while providing limited insight into how visual evidence contributes to location predictions. This study presents an explainable country level image geolocation pipeline for 11 ASEAN countries. First, we collected 4,850 images from GeoGuessr style sources, Google Images, and additional street level imagery. We then evaluated three approaches on this dataset: CLIP zero shot classification, a LightGBM classifier, and an MLP classifier. The MLP achieved the best test performance, attaining an accuracy and F1 score of 85.91%. For explainability, predictions generated by the MLP classifier were analyzed post hoc using CLIP attention rollout, YOLO26 object detection on the original images, and Energy Based Pointing Game (EBPG) overlap metrics. Object level analysis indicates that frequently detected objects are not necessarily associated with the highest attention density, suggesting that object frequency and attention based visual evidence capture different aspects of a scene. These results demonstrate that the proposed model can support accurate regional image geolocation while enabling object level inspection of the visual cues underlying its predictions.

AI.tldr: The study introduces an explainable MLP-based country-level image geolocation pipeline for 11 ASEAN countries, achieving 85.91% accuracy and F1 score. Post hoc analysis evaluates visual cues using CLIP, YOLO, and EBPG metrics.
AI.motivation: Geolocation using visual content alone can be difficult in regions with similar visual characteristics, limiting insights into how visual evidence informs predictions.
AI.method: The authors collected 4,850 images and applied CLIP zero-shot classification, LightGBM, and MLP classifier models to analyze and predict geolocation, with post hoc analysis enhancing explainability.
AI.result: The MLP classifier performed best among three approaches, attaining high geolocation accuracy and F1 scores. Attention density and object frequency captured distinct aspects during object-level analysis.
AI.conclusion: The model achieves competitive geolocation performance while enabling explainability regarding object-level visual cues that influence predictions.

title: Autonomous Tracking and Terminal Guidance of Moving Targets for Fixed-Wing UAVs
summary: This study introduces a unified control framework for fixed-wing unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) fitted with a pan-tilt (PT) camera, intended to perform an end-to-end mission spanning from initial target detection to accurate terminal engagement. The proposed system employs a three-phase strategy: a vision-based target acquisition phase, an NMPC-based tracking phase, and a terminal guidance phase. During tracking, the framework uses an Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) to fuse YOLO-based visual detections with inertial measurements, enabling robust target state estimation under unknown dynamics. To ensure reliable visual contact, we introduce a constraint-aware Nonlinear Model Predictive Control (NMPC) strategy that incorporates Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) to explicitly prevent UAV self-occlusion -- a common limitation in fixed-wing tracking. Upon satisfying terminal engagement conditions, the system seamlessly transitions control to a quaternion-based Biased Proportional Navigation Guidance (BPNG) law, enforcing precise impact angle constraints. High-fidelity simulations demonstrate that the framework achieves stable, robust tracking and accurate terminal interception while strictly respecting the vehicle's dynamic limits and camera field-of-view constraints.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a robust unified control framework for fixed-wing UAVs with a PT camera to handle end-to-end missions, from detecting to engaging a target, using a three-phase approach combining vision, tracking, and terminal guidance.
AI.motivation: The research aims to address challenges in using UAVs with PT cameras for accurate target engagement, including robust target tracking under unknown conditions, preventing self-occlusion, and achieving precise terminal guidance with dynamic constraints.
AI.method: The study introduces a three-phase system: vision-based target acquisition; NMPC tracking that incorporates UKF for sensor fusion and CBFs to avoid occlusion; and quaternion-based BPNG for terminal guidance to satisfy impact angle constraints.
AI.result: Simulation results show the framework effectively performs robust tracking, maintains visual contact, and ensures precise terminal interception without violating UAV dynamic and field-of-view limitations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully integrates vision, control, and guidance systems to provide a reliable solution for fixed-wing UAV missions, marking significant advancements in UAV target engagement capabilities.

title: Mitigating The Effect of Class Imbalance in Data with Hierarchical and Dependable Structure
comment: 8 pages, 2 figures, 4 tables; preprint submitted to IEEE COMPSAC 2026
summary: Classifying cybersecurity vulnerabilities using the Common Weakness Enumeration (CWE) taxonomy is challenging due to extreme class imbalance and strong hierarchical dependencies among weakness categories. Although oversampling techniques such as Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) and Adaptive Synthetic Sampling (ADASYN) are widely adopted to mitigate class imbalance, their effectiveness for hierarchical CWE text classification remains largely unexplored. This paper proposes a Hierarchy-Aware RoBERTa framework that explicitly incorporates CWE structural information through learnable parent-class embeddings, preserving taxonomic consistency. Our experiments demonstrate that synthetic interpolation in high-dimensional embedding spaces violates the inherent parent-child constraints of the CWE hierarchy, offering only marginal benefits for classical ML models while consistently degrading deep learning architectures. Evaluated on a CWE Research Concept dataset, the proposed model achieves a weighted F1-score of 0.76 without data augmentation, outperforming all baselines with notable gains on minority classes, including the Class category whose F1-score improved from 0.40 to 0.60 over the BERT baseline. Our results suggest that hierarchy-aware representation learning is a more principled alternative to oversampling for structured vulnerability classification.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the challenge in classifying cybersecurity vulnerabilities using the hierarchical Common Weakness Enumeration (CWE) taxonomy, proposing a Hierarchy-Aware RoBERTa method that outperforms traditional oversampling techniques.
AI.motivation: Classifying cybersecurity vulnerabilities is difficult due to class imbalance and hierarchical dependencies in the CWE taxonomy.
AI.method: Introduces a Hierarchy-Aware RoBERTa framework that incorporates structural CWE hierarchy using learnable parent-class embeddings.
AI.result: Achieved a weighted F1-score of 0.76 without data augmentation, significantly improving performance on minority classes compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchy-aware representation learning is a more effective approach than oversampling for structured vulnerability classification.

title: From Observation to Insight: Mechanistic World Models and the Quest for Autonomous Discovery
summary: Recent advances in foundation models have transformed AI for Science, enabling remarkably accurate predictive performance across domains ranging from protein folding to weather forecasting. Yet prediction alone does not constitute scientific discovery. Scientific understanding depends on uncovering the reusable explanatory mechanisms that generate observations, whereas contemporary machine learning remains fundamentally organised around predictive mappings rather than explanatory structure. In this paper, we argue that scientific discovery is fundamentally a problem of knowledge organisation. To this end, we introduce Mechanistic World Models, a new design paradigm that places reusable mechanisms at the centre of representation, computation and learning. Drawing on insights from the philosophy of science, we derive the computational capabilities required for discovery, identify the design principles and inductive pressures that encourage explanatory knowledge to emerge, and formalise the anatomy of a mechanism-centric world model. Finally, we show how diverse research directions including mechanistic interpretability, causal representation learning, equation discovery and modular architectures capture complementary ingredients of this paradigm while lacking a unified framework. We propose Mechanistic World Models as a conceptual foundation and computational blueprint for moving AI beyond predictive forecasting towards autonomous scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Mechanistic World Models as a paradigm for advancing AI from predictive capabilities to autonomous scientific discovery, focusing on reusable explanatory mechanisms.
AI.motivation: AI has achieved remarkable predictive accuracy in science but lacks the ability to generate scientific understanding by uncovering explanatory mechanisms.
AI.method: Proposes Mechanistic World Models, a paradigm centered on reusable explanatory mechanisms, supported by design principles and insights from philosophy of science, alongside a formal framework.
AI.result: Identifies computational requirements and shows how diverse research areas contribute to this paradigm but lack a unified framework.
AI.conclusion: Mechanistic World Models can serve as a foundation for AI to transition from predictive forecasting to achieving autonomous scientific discovery.

title: Evaluating Health Misinformation in Low-Resource Languages: Integrating Small Language Models with a Culturally-Sensitive Responsible NLP Framework (Bangla as a Case Study)
summary: Artificial Intelligence (AI) technologies, while serving as a foundational enabler for modern social media and digital health services, exert a bivalent effect by simultaneously acting as a combatant against and a spread vector for misinformation. A prevalent challenge in mitigating this issue arises in non-English contexts and low socioeconomic classes, where limited data hinders the training of AI models for effective detection. Consequently, culturally and linguistically diverse (CALD) communities struggle to access trustworthy health information through AI-driven tools. Current AI tools underperform due to a lack of training data and are largely unable to consider language nuances and traditions in non-English contexts. This research addresses these gaps by proposing a CALD-friendly AI-based health misinformation detector and providing a dashboard for medical professionals to analyse this misinformation, a critical step toward mitigating a growing concern among CALD populations. To this end, we conduct a series of experiments using a Bangla-translated health misinformation dataset to evaluate the performance of various Small Language Models (SLMs). SLMs are particularly relevant in this context given the frequent underperformance of Large Language Models (LLMs), which often stems from insufficient domain-specific knowledge and the prohibitive costs of resource-intensive fine-tuning. The results demonstrate that Phi-4 is the superior model, achieving an ideal balance between precision and recall in claim extraction. Then, to mitigate the limitations of SLMs, we design and test a novel health misinformation detection framework grounded in Responsible Natural Language Processing (NLP), which incorporates cultural sensitivity, potential for harm, and communication quality, thereby providing a holistic lens for evaluating misinformation in low-resource languages.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a novel AI-based framework to combat health misinformation in culturally diverse contexts, focusing on low-resource languages.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of effective AI tools for identifying health misinformation in non-English and low-socioeconomic contexts, where traditional models falter due to insufficient training data and sensitivity to cultural nuances.
AI.method: Small Language Models (SLMs) were evaluated on a Bangla-translated health misinformation dataset, and a new health misinformation detection framework emphasizing Responsible NLP principles was developed and tested.
AI.result: Phi-4 was identified as the best-performing Small Language Model, achieving a good balance between precision and recall. The newly proposed framework incorporates cultural sensitivity, harm mitigation, and effective communication.
AI.conclusion: The study provides a CALD-friendly approach to health misinformation detection, emphasizing the importance of tailoring AI tools to diverse linguistic and cultural contexts and addressing data limitations effectively.

title: Cross-Cutting Security Analysis of LLM-Generated Code via Metamorphic Testing and Association Rule Mining
comment: This work has already been accepted by IEEE 27th International Conference on Information Reuse and Integration for Data Science
summary: Large language models (LLMs) frequently generate code with security vulnerabilities, yet these weaknesses are rarely isolated: they often span multiple concern areas simultaneously, reflecting the cross-cutting nature of security in software. We present a framework that combines security-oriented Metamorphic Relations (MRs) with Association Rule (AR) mining to detect vulnerabilities in LLM-generated code, uncover their co-violation structure, and trace that structure back to prompt-level risk factors. We define nine MRs covering major CWE categories, including SQL injection, XSS, command injection, path traversal, hard-coded credentials, weak cryptography, and memory-safety errors, and apply them using an LLM-based judge to 3,700 code snippets generated by five open models from the LLMSecEval benchmark. The results show that 68.8% of snippets violate at least one MR, with hard-coded credentials (79.1%) and command injection (74.4%) among the most prevalent applicable failures. AR mining reveals strong cross-cutting co-violation patterns, notably that XSS and weak cryptography co-violations predict hard-coded credentials with 82.5% confidence (lift = 3.23), along with tightly coupled clusters linking authentication, credential handling, and cryptographic weakness, as well as input-handling and memory-safety failures. We then perform prompt-level risk analysis and find that database- and authentication-related prompts are strong predictors of broad cross-cutting insecurity, while 65.5% of prompts yield consistent violation outcomes across all five models. These findings show that insecure code generation is not merely a collection of independent defects, but a structured and prompt-conditioned phenomenon, motivating cluster-aware verification and prompt-level intervention for safer LLM-assisted programming.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a framework to detect security vulnerabilities in LLM-generated code by combining security-oriented metamorphic relations (MRs) and association rule (AR) mining, finding widespread structured and prompt-conditioned insecurity.
AI.motivation: To address the systemic security vulnerabilities in code generated by large language models (LLMs), cross-cutting insecurities, and understanding these risks in a structured and predictive manner.
AI.method: Defined nine security-focused metamorphic relations (MRs), applied them to 3,700 LLM-generated code snippets, and used association rule mining to analyze co-violation structures and trace prompt-level risks.
AI.result: 68.8% of the analyzed snippets violated at least one MR, with significant co-violation patterns like XSS and weak cryptography being predictive of hard-coded credentials. Certain prompt types strongly correlate with broader insecurity.
AI.conclusion: Insecurity in LLM-generated code is systematic and structured, not random. This necessitates cluster-aware verification frameworks and prompt-level intervention to enhance security in LLM-assisted coding.

title: Semantic-Edge Response Decoding of SAM3 for Zero-Shot Crack Segmentation
summary: Crack segmentation is essential for infrastructure inspection and structural health assessment, but existing high-performance methods typically require task-specific pixel-level annotations and training. Text-promptable vision foundation models enable zero-shot deployment, yet their final mask proposals are poorly suited to thin, fragmented, and low-contrast cracks, whose evidence may be suppressed, truncated, or over-expanded during mask generation. We find that language-conditioned semantic responses within the SAM3 decoder preserve more continuous and complete crack evidence than its final masks. Based on this observation, we propose Semantic-Edge Response Decoding (SERD), which interprets internal responses as a dense crack-likelihood field, calibrates them with a lightweight edge prior, and generates crack masks using a unified global threshold, without annotation or fine-tuning. Experiments on six public datasets show that SERD consistently improves over native SAM3 and outperforms the compared zero-shot and open-vocabulary segmentation methods, achieving an average Crack IoU of 61.14\%, 4.63 points higher than SAM3. Further analyses show that most gains arise from directly decoding internal semantic responses, while edge calibration improves structural recovery and false-positive control without increasing end-to-end inference overhead. These results suggest that, for thin and non-compact targets, internal continuous responses can provide a more transferable interface than the final masks of foundation models. Code is available at: https://github.com/xauat-liushipeng/SERD

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Semantic-Edge Response Decoding (SERD), a method for crack segmentation that avoids manual annotation and fine-tuning by leveraging internal semantic responses from a vision model, improving segmentation performance significantly.
AI.motivation: Existing crack segmentation methods require extensive pixel-level annotation and training, making them impractical for efficient deployment. Vision foundation models offer a zero-shot solution, but their mask generation struggles with thin and fragmented cracks.
AI.method: SERD utilizes language-conditioned semantic responses from the SAM3 decoder to construct a dense crack-likelihood field, calibrates these responses with an edge prior, and generates masks using a global threshold. This approach avoids annotations and retraining.
AI.result: SERD outperforms existing zero-shot segmentation methods, including SAM3, by achieving an average Crack IoU of 61.14%, with significant improvements in structural recovery and false-positive control.
AI.conclusion: For thin, fragmented, low-contrast cracks, decoding continuous semantic responses within foundation models is more effective and transferable than relying on their final mask outputs. SERD facilitates better zero-shot crack segmentation without increasing computational overhead.

title: PixelLoop: Shortcut Topological Navigation with Pixel-Level Loops
comment: 2026 IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS); 8 pages, 5 figures
summary: Although topological mapping and navigation have been studied extensively, the specific role and downstream effect of loop closures in purely topological representations has received relatively little attention. Importantly, loop closure over topological maps is distinct from loop closure over globally referenced trajectories and metric maps. Building on recent denser topologies grounded in pixel-level, relative 3D geometry, we propose PixelLoop which introduces loop closures directly in pixel space. Unlike sparse image-level edges or pose-graph corrections in SLAM, our pixel-level closures act as dense topological shortcuts that alter planning connectivity and cost propagation rather than merely aligning coordinates. This dense connectivity enables stable any-point-to-any-point navigation and produces costmaps that align accurately with geometric shortest paths. In particular, we showcase the distinct advantage of applying loop closures to fine-grained pixel topologies rather than image-level topologies. Across extensive simulated experiments, PixelLoop achieves over 35% absolute improvement in both Success Rate and SPL compared to image-relative baselines, with the largest gains in scenarios requiring shortcut exploitation. Results are further validated through real-world mobile robot deployments, demonstrating that dense pixel-level loop closures provide a practical and robust foundation for topological visual navigation. Project Page: https://pixelloop-nav.github.io/

AI.tldr: This paper introduces PixelLoop, a method for topological navigation that uses dense pixel-level loop closures for improved planning and navigation performance.
AI.motivation: To explore the role and impact of loop closures on topological maps, especially focusing on fine-grained, pixel-level geometries, to improve navigation and planning.
AI.method: PixelLoop employs dense pixel-level loop closures as topological shortcuts, which alter planning connectivity and cost propagation, shifting away from traditional sparse image-level approaches.
AI.result: PixelLoop achieves over 35% improvement in Success Rate and SPL in simulations, with significant gains in environments requiring shortcut exploitation. Real-world deployments validate its robustness.
AI.conclusion: Dense pixel-level loop closures provide a practical, efficient, and accurate approach for topological navigation, offering substantial improvements over image-level baselines.

title: TRACE: An Operational Reasoning Schema for Auditable Agentic Commitments
summary: This paper defines TRACE (Typed Reasoning And Commitment Evidence): a typed, versioned schema for recording reasoning traces, a reference procedure for writing records against it, and one operating discipline, no durable state change without a record. The paper argues in three layers that reasoning is not in the language model: the autoregressive mechanism natively computes association; chain-of-thought and reinforcement learning inherit its limits; and the formal constructs of reasoning theory, from Socratic procedure to Pearl's ladder, are absent as machinery. The schema answers the absence with fields and tests: the TraceRecord and its causal specialization, an eight-stage reference writer, a gate-first measurement regime, the TRACE-Bench protocol, and the consumers, memory admission, plan gating, temporal regret, and verdict reuse, whose more auditable decisions are the measure of the record. A record-consumer contract states what a record guarantees and what a consumer must honor in return, making the schema an operational interface rather than a passive document. Two worked examples run in the main text: a music-lessons argument traced from sentence to typed verdict, separating association, intervention, and prescription; and a flood search-and-rescue vignette in which a predictive world model reports confident plan success that its own support and out-of-distribution scores contradict, so the record defers the commitment, requests a bounded observation, revises append-only, and clears a different branch. The vignette is illustrative, not empirical; closed-loop evaluation is left to future work, so the contribution is the schema and its contract, not a performance claim. Appendices carry the full schema, writer algorithms and cost model, clinical and policy illustrations, the benchmark protocol, convergence metrics, and usage scenarios.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces TRACE, a typed, versioned schema for documenting reasoning traces, alongside reference procedures and an operational discipline for accountable reasoning. It highlights how reasoning is distinct from language models and lacks direct machinery within them, proposing TRACE as a structured solution.
AI.motivation: To address the absence of formal reasoning capabilities in autoregressive language models and provide a structured framework (TRACE) for documenting and evaluating reasoning traces and decisions.
AI.method: The authors propose TRACE, including schema definitions (e.g., TraceRecord), an eight-stage reference writer, and supporting fields/tests. They present TRACE-Bench protocols, operational interfaces for decision-making, and worked examples to illustrate its use in real-world reasoning scenarios.
AI.result: The paper does not provide empirical performance evaluations but demonstrates TRACE’s applications through examples, emphasizing its potential for making reasoning processes auditable and accountable.
AI.conclusion: TRACE offers a structured, operational approach to tracing reasoning and decision-making, but its practical effectiveness requires future closed-loop evaluations. The contribution is primarily conceptual and framework-oriented at this stage.

title: Policy-Conditioned Constrained Decoding for Column-Level Access Control in Text-to-SQL
summary: Text-to-SQL is increasingly deployed across trust boundaries between data providers and users. Such deployment must balance three competing requirements: policy compliance, answer coverage, and bounded cost. Existing approaches typically decide refusal based on which columns a query mentions and enforce it stochastically. Whether a query is compliant, however, depends not only on which columns appear but on how they are used, and stochastic enforcement cannot deterministically rule out violations. We formalize this requirement as a column-use policy over semantic use: output, filter condition, and aggregation argument. We integrate the policy by aligning each role with grammar productions tracked by the decoder. The resulting system, PCC-SQL, applies a per-token logits mask that deterministically eliminates single-query column-use violations on the supported SQL fragment in a single decoding pass. Across three benchmarks and three open-source models, PCC-SQL achieves 0% Leakage Rate and Coverage up to 88.7% on Spider-CU, while staying within +10% tokens of direct prompting. We additionally assess semantic alignment with execution accuracy.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PCC-SQL, a system that enforces column-use policies in Text-to-SQL queries deterministically during decoding, achieving high compliance and coverage across evaluations.
AI.motivation: Text-to-SQL must operate across trust boundaries, balancing policy compliance, answer coverage, and limited cost. Existing stochastic methods fail to fully prevent violations or consider semantic column use, necessitating precise control over policy adherence.
AI.method: The paper formalizes column-use policies based on semantic roles like output, filter conditions, and aggregation arguments. It integrates policy enforcement into the grammar, applying token-based logits masking during decoding to prevent rule violations deterministically.
AI.result: PCC-SQL achieves a 0% leakage rate and maximum coverage of 88.7% on benchmarks like Spider-CU, with minimal increase in token count (+10%) compared to direct prompting.
AI.conclusion: PCC-SQL successfully enforces deterministic compliance with column-use policies, offering a robust solution for secure, policy-aligned Text-to-SQL deployment without sacrificing efficiency.

title: Adaptive Cross-Modal Fusion with Sparse Attention for Pedestrian Crossing Intention Prediction
comment: 17 pages, 5 figures, 4 tables. Under review at PeerJ Computer Science
summary: Predicting pedestrian crossing intention is a safety-critical task for autonomous driving, yet existing approaches often rely on single-modal inputs or dense multimodal fusion strategies that inadequately capture complementary visual and kinematic information while introducing redundant inter-modal interactions. We propose ADAPT (Adaptive Domain-Aware Pedestrian Crossing Transformer), a multimodal framework that jointly models local and global visual context together with temporal motion dynamics for accurate pedestrian crossing intention prediction. ADAPT processes four spatially aligned visual modalities, including RGB images, local depth maps, global semantic maps, and global depth maps, together with ego-vehicle speed, pedestrian bounding boxes, and skeleton pose information through five specialized modules: a weight-shared Swin Transformer V2 backbone for visual feature extraction, a Cross-Modality Guided Attention module for hierarchical visual fusion, a Mamba-based Motion Feature Encoding module for efficient temporal modeling, a Sparse Cross-Modal Attention module that selectively preserves the most informative inter-modal interactions, and a Vision Transformer-based Temporal Feature Fusion module for sequence-level prediction. Extensive experiments on the JAAD and PIE benchmark datasets demonstrate that ADAPT consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods while maintaining low computational complexity. On JAAD, the proposed method achieves an AUC of 0.73 on JAADbeh and 0.85 on JAADall, while on PIE it achieves an accuracy of 0.92 and an AUC of 0.90. Furthermore, ADAPT performs inference in only 17.23 ms per sample, offering an effective balance between predictive accuracy and real-time deployment efficiency for intelligent transportation and autonomous driving applications.

AI.tldr: ADAPT is a multimodal framework for predicting pedestrian crossing intention accurately and efficiently, using visual and kinematic data.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of single-modal inputs and dense multimodal fusion strategies in capturing complementary visual and kinematic data for pedestrian intention prediction.
AI.method: ADAPT leverages four visual modalities and ego-vehicle data processed through five specialized modules, including Swin Transformer V2, hierarchical and sparse attention mechanisms, and temporal modeling techniques.
AI.result: ADAPT outperforms state-of-the-art methods on JAAD and PIE datasets, achieving superior AUC and accuracy scores while maintaining a computational inference time of 17.23 ms per sample.
AI.conclusion: ADAPT provides an effective balance between high predictive accuracy and real-time efficiency, making it highly suitable for autonomous driving applications.

title: Unveiling Complex Collective Behaviors from Simple Rewards
summary: Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) holds great potential for robot swarms, but the black-box nature of neural policies complicates strategic analysis, limiting multi-robot applications. Furthermore, complex swarm behaviors can surprisingly emerge from simple rewards without explicit aggregation incentives. Unveiling the mechanisms behind this emergence is critical, but the disconnection between simple rewards and collective behaviors exacerbates interpretability challenges. This paper aims to reveal the hidden mechanisms in this process. We propose a two-stage EEC (\LinkIII) explanatory framework. This includes a novel analytical tool called the Agent Response Map (ARM), which reveals agents' decision-making patterns across space and identifies regions of aggregation and avoidance. ARM reveals that the robots implicitly learn the geometric fields of the environment and utilize these structures as desired targets for coordinated movement. We validate this finding across two distinct tasks: a cooperative multi-robot shape assembly and a competitive predator-prey pursuit-evasion. 1) In the cooperative task, ARM identifies the unoccupied target interior as the desired destination for robot navigation. As the center becomes occupied, this target region automatically shifts toward the boundary, demonstrating the robots' capacity to autonomously explore unoccupied areas. 2) In the competitive task, ARM surprisingly identifies the boundary of the predators' Voronoi diagram as the convergence destination for prey agents. Together, these two tasks demonstrate the capability of ARM to discover the hidden geometric structures underlying MARL policies in robot swarms.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an explanatory tool to interpret the behaviors of multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) in robot swarms.
AI.motivation: To address the interpretability challenges posed by the black-box nature of neural policies in MARL, which complicate strategic analysis and hinder the deployment of collective robot behaviors.
AI.method: The authors propose a two-stage EEC framework with the introduction of the "Agent Response Map" (ARM), designed to reveal decision-making patterns and spatial behavior dynamics of agents in robot swarms.
AI.result: ARM is validated in two tasks: cooperative shape assembly and competitive pursuit-evasion. The tool identifies the target navigation regions in the cooperative task and reveals prey's convergence to the predators' Voronoi diagram boundary.
AI.conclusion: ARM effectively uncovers hidden geometric structures in MARL policies, providing insights into the mechanisms behind swarm behavior and revealing how agents learn spatial structures from simple rewards.

title: Institutional Equity Holdings Prediction Using Node Affinities of Dynamic Graphs
summary: Institutional equity holdings disclosed in SEC Form 13F filings provide a rich temporal record of portfolio decisions by large investment managers. However, forecasting future allocations and modeling future demand remains challenging due to disclosure lags, reporting noise, and strong persistence in institutional behavior. We introduce the first benchmark for these tasks using temporal graph machine learning, framing holdings prediction as node affinity prediction -- i.e., forecasting portfolio weights -- on a discrete-time temporal bipartite graph of managers and securities extracted from preprocessed filings. On a sampled dataset comprising 99 managers and the S\&P 500 index (503 securities, 209,351 temporal edges across 48 quarters from 2013--2025), Node Affinity prediction model using Virtual State (NAVIS) achieves a state-of-the-art test Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG) of 0.9127 with features (0.9121 without), outperforming all dynamic graph representation learning competitors by a substantial margin, and outperforming all heuristic methods. Remarkably, a simple Exponential Moving Average baseline achieves 0.8882, surpassing all dynamic graph models except NAVIS and all heuristics except Persistent Forecast (0.8891), highlighting the strong smoothness and persistence of institutional portfolios. Domain-specific node features provide only marginal gains (<1.2\%), indicating that temporal and structural signals in the 13F ownership graph already capture most of the predictable information. By benchmarking a suite of Temporal Graph Benchmark (TGB) models under the node affinity prediction setting, both with and without features, on real-world 13F data, this work provides a reproducible foundation for temporal graph machine learning in holdings prediction and portfolio allocation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces NAVIS, a temporal graph learning model for predicting institutional equity holdings, which achieves state-of-the-art performance in forecasting portfolio weights using SEC Form 13F filings.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address the challenges of forecasting portfolio allocations given the strong persistence in institutional behavior, disclosure lags, and reporting noise in SEC Form 13F filings.
AI.method: A novel temporal graph machine learning framework, NAVIS, predicts portfolio weights by treating the holdings data as a discrete-time temporal bipartite graph. NAVIS is benchmarked against various methods using a sampled dataset of institutional equity holdings.
AI.result: NAVIS achieves a state-of-the-art NDCG score of 0.9127 (with features) and significantly outperforms dynamic graph models and heuristic methods on holdings prediction tasks. Domain-specific features add minimal improvement.
AI.conclusion: Temporal and structural signals in institutional holdings are highly predictive. NAVIS and the study's benchmarks establish a robust base for forecasting future allocations using temporal graph methods.

title: The Model Knows Your Project, Not You: Measuring Recognition in LLMs with NameRank
summary: What a frontier model recalls about a person or tool from its own weights -- before any retrieval step -- often shapes the first description a human sees, making that parametric corpus presence a measurement problem. Citations explain about a third of whether a model recognizes a researcher; we target the residual and build NameRank, a [0,1] recognition score: each of 4,685 entities in 54 cohorts is probed with one open-ended question across 36 models, and an independent judge returns a binary verdict against a curated gold -- did the model state a specific, non-guessable fact about this exact entity? -- so hallucination, context echo, and guesses earn nothing. Synthetic-null entities hold the floor near zero, and verdicts track the entity, not the model. One thesis organizes the findings: recognition is paid to named, indexable artifacts, not to credentials or titles. Every Olympic-style credential sits below a working-researcher baseline, because no named artifact ships with the medal, yet the ranking inverts at the marquee tier, where Nobel, Turing, and Fields laureates saturate the panel. For independent creators the tool out-ranks its maker, and the credential that does propagate is a named method or awarded paper. Being one of many named contributors to a celebrated artifact, by contrast, earns almost nothing -- the authors listed on a flagship model report or system card sit near the recognition floor -- because recognition attaches to the artifact's own distinctive name, not to the roster behind it. No bibliometric predicts recognition well; top-density institutions out-recognize peers at matched citations; and on 258 news events recognition loads on peak salience, not persistence. A self-report probe shows introspection reads a corpus prior, not its own knowledge.

AI.tldr: The study examines recognition scores for entities based on AI models' parametric memories, focusing on named artifact recognition above individual credentials. Nobel-tier achievers rank highly, while group contributors often get low recognition.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to analyze how AI models recognize entities and what factors influence recognition, especially when retrieval is absent.
AI.method: Entities were probed with an open-ended question across 36 models, judged independently for accuracy against a curated standard, excluding guesses or hallucinations.
AI.result: Recognition was mostly tied to unique, named artifacts rather than individual credentials or affiliations. Top-tier awards and methods influenced recognition more than group contributions or bibliometric measures.
AI.conclusion: AI models prioritize named entities and artifacts over individual achievements or institutional reputation for recognition, indicating a shift in measure for acknowledgment in parametric memories.

title: Beyond Binary Detection: A Multi-Dimensional Taxonomy of Cancer Misinformation on Reddit
summary: Cancer-related discussions on social media provide an important space for information exchange and peer support, but also facilitate the spread of misinformation that may influence prevention, screening, and treatment decisions. Existing research on cancer misinformation often relies on narrow definitions, small-scale datasets, or binary labeling frameworks. We introduce a multi-dimensional taxonomy for characterizing cancer misinformation in Reddit discussions of breast, lung, colon, and prostate cancer. The taxonomy captures seven dimensions, including misinformation presence, information type, risk level, stance, and topical focus. Using expert-annotated data, we evaluate multiple large language models (LLMs) for scalable misinformation annotation and analyze cancer misinformation across Reddit communities. Our results show that cancer-related misinformation constitutes approximately 6\% of Reddit cancer discussions, with substantial variation across communities and misinformation topics. Few-shot prompting substantially improves classification performance, particularly for nuanced taxonomy dimensions. We additionally identify recurring misinformation narratives centered on unsupported treatments, distrust of conventional medicine, and misleading claims about diagnosis and screening. Our taxonomy, dataset, and findings provide a foundation for multi-dimensional modeling of online cancer misinformation.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a multi-dimensional taxonomy to study cancer-related misinformation in Reddit discussions, evaluates large language models for misinformation annotation, and reveals insights about prevalence and themes of misinformation in discussions.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind this research is to address narrow definitions, small datasets, and simplistic labeling frameworks in existing cancer misinformation studies while providing tools to better understand and counter misinformation.
AI.method: The paper introduces a seven-dimension taxonomy, employs expert annotation of data, and evaluates large language models using techniques like few-shot prompting to ensure scalable annotation.
AI.result: Approximately 6% of cancer discussions on Reddit contain misinformation, with key themes on unsupported treatments, distrust of conventional medicine, and misleading claims about diagnosis and screening. Few-shot prompting enhances classification performance.
AI.conclusion: The study contributes a robust taxonomy, dataset, and insights for modeling cancer misinformation, laying the groundwork for nuanced and scalable analysis in future research.

title: MobileSAM2: Lightweight Segment Anything for Spatial Intelligence
summary: The recent large video foundation model, SAM2, enables segment anything in both images and videos, serving as a powerful base model for various applications. However, many of such use cases require to operate on resource-constrained devices like mobile phones and laptops. In this work, we aim to make SAM2 more mobile-friendly by distilling the heavyweight SAM2 into a lightweight model, facilitating segment anything in both images and videos on mobile devices. To this end, we propose Hypergraphical Knowledge Distill (HyperKD), which introduces the idea of hypergraph into knowledge distillation, aiming to effectively model and transfer SAM2's generalizable and comprehensive knowledge. HyperKD consists of Temporal HyperKD and Granularity HyperKD that construct hypergraphs to explicitly model and extract the generalizable temporal knowledge and the comprehensive multi-granularity knowledge from SAM2 respectively, which are then distilled into the lightweight student model by aligning it with the constructed hypergraphs. Besides, we present MobileSAM2, a new family of lightweight SAM2 that balances efficiency and effectiveness via searching the best model architectures with HyperKD during model size reduction. Extensive experiments validate MobileSAM2 across multiple benchmarks and show promising generalization performance on embodied AI tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MobileSAM2, a mobile-optimized lightweight version of the video segmentation model SAM2, utilizing a novel Hypergraphical Knowledge Distillation (HyperKD) technique.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to adapt the computationally intensive SAM2 model so it can operate efficiently on resource-constrained devices like mobile phones and laptops, thereby broadening its usability.
AI.method: The authors introduce HyperKD, a novel knowledge distillation method that leverages hypergraphs to model and transfer knowledge from SAM2 to a lightweight student model. Temporal and Granularity HyperKD mechanisms are used to transfer generalizable temporal knowledge and multi-granularity knowledge, respectively.
AI.result: They achieve a new family of lightweight models called MobileSAM2, which demonstrates efficient and effective performance. Experiments show promising results across multiple benchmarks, including embodied AI tasks.
AI.conclusion: HyperKD effectively enables the distillation of SAM2 into the lightweight MobileSAM2 models, which balance efficiency and performance, making them suitable for use on mobile and resource-limited devices.

title: UR-VC: Unsupervised Robotic Value Correction for Time-Derived Progress Proxies
summary: Modern robot learning systems increasingly rely on dense progress or value signals to evaluate intermediate states, guide policy learning, and detect task completion, making the quality of these signals critical. Since such dense labels are rarely available at scale, normalized time within a demonstration is often used as a scalable substitute: later frames are treated as higher progress. However, this time-derived label is only a noisy proxy for physical task progress. In contact-rich manipulation, a robot may make progress and then lose it through slips, failed grasps, or partial undoing, while the time-derived label continues to increase monotonically. We introduce Unsupervised Robotic Value Correction (UR-VC), an offline, training-free method for correcting time-derived progress labels. UR-VC exploits a simple regularity in demonstration data: similar states often recur across different episodes, but at different timestamps. Instead of trusting the timestamp from a single trajectory, UR-VC retrieves similar states from other episodes and aggregates their time-derived labels to obtain a corrected progress estimate. UR-VC requires no manual progress labels, reward annotations, or additional value model. We evaluate UR-VC on real bimanual cloth flatten-and-fold data, a long-horizon deformable-object manipulation task with visible intermediate progress. The corrected labels capture local regressions and non-uniform progress that normalized time cannot represent, while preserving the overall task trend. We further use the corrected signal to construct advantage labels for VLA training, following recent advantage-conditioned policy learning. UR-VC shows a positive trend in real-robot task success under matched data, model, and training settings.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Unsupervised Robotic Value Correction (UR-VC), a method to refine time-derived progress labels for more accurate evaluation in robotic manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: Dense progress signals, often derived from normalized time within demonstrations, misrepresent actual task progress, especially in contact-rich manipulation tasks.
AI.method: UR-VC uses similar states across different episodes to aggregate time-derived labels and correct progress estimates. It operates offline without requiring manual labels, reward annotations, or additional models.
AI.result: Evaluation on a cloth manipulation task shows UR-VC's corrected signals address local regressions and non-uniform progress, while improving real-robot task success rates.
AI.conclusion: UR-VC enhances task progress estimation and improves policy learning without manual labeling, offering benefits in long-horizon, deformable-object manipulation tasks.

title: Sparse Autoencoders for Interpretable Out-of-Distribution Detection
summary: Reliable detection of out-of-distribution (OOD) samples is crucial for the safe deployment of machine learning models. Neural networks often produce overconfident predictions for inputs that deviate from their training data, leading to significant degradation in performance. While many OOD detection methods focus on the final output layer, they neglect the rich hierarchical information present in intermediate network layers. This paper introduces a novel approach that leverages sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to learn interpretable features from these intermediate activations. We find that in-distribution (ID) and OOD data activate distinct sets of these sparse features. We propose a new OOD score derived from the cosine similarity between the sparse feature activations of a test sample and the mean activations of ID classes. Our post-hoc detection method not only achieves state-of-the-art performance on standard OOD detection benchmarks, but yields interpretable insights into how distribution shift affects learned representations.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes using sparse autoencoders for OOD detection by analyzing intermediate activations, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Ensure reliable identification of OOD samples for safer machine learning model deployment.
AI.method: Uses sparse autoencoders to study intermediate network layer activations and introduces a new score based on cosine similarity.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on OOD detection benchmarks with interpretable features.
AI.conclusion: Sparse feature activations can distinguish ID and OOD data effectively, highlighting how distribution shifts change representations.

title: Evidence-Grounded AI for Musculoskeletal Care
summary: Musculoskeletal diseases are among the leading causes of disability worldwide and create the greatest global need for rehabilitation. Because recovery, remodelling and degeneration often unfold over months to years, musculoskeletal care requires longitudinal management that repeatedly integrates evolving patient evidence, external medical knowledge and stage-specific functional goals. In routine practice, this evidence is fragmented across visits, departments and hospital systems, limiting individualized, evidence-based care. Here we report OrthoPilot, a clinical artificial intelligence system powered by a large language model that integrates hospital data streams with authoritative external knowledge for continuous musculoskeletal management. OrthoPilot autonomously retrieves real-time imaging, laboratory, pathology and order data and converts evolving patient states into evidence-based decisions from admission diagnosis to rehabilitation planning. We established a specialist-validated benchmark from real-world electronic health records spanning 1,000 disease codes. In a reader study across the complete care pathway, OrthoPilot was compared with 81 orthopaedic physicians and surpassed experts with 25 years of experience in diagnostic reasoning, clinical decision-making and management planning. It also outperformed all evaluated intelligent systems across 60 external clinical centres. In a prospective study of 1,870 complex cases, OrthoPilot increased full-chain management success by 10.6%. During an 8-month randomised deployment involving 8,240 inpatients, it increased cumulative cases per bed by 9.7% and improved patient-reported access to health information. These results move clinical AI from predicting isolated events toward executing longitudinal management across complete musculoskeletal care pathways.

AI.tldr: OrthoPilot, an AI system, streamlines musculoskeletal care by integrating hospital data with external knowledge, surpassing experienced doctors in clinical decision-making, and improving care pathway outcomes.
AI.motivation: Musculoskeletal diseases require long-term management, but current care is fragmented, leading to suboptimal individualized and evidence-based treatment.
AI.method: Developed OrthoPilot, an AI system using a large language model, to autonomously integrate real-time patient data and external knowledge for managing the full musculoskeletal care pathway.
AI.result: OrthoPilot surpassed expert physicians in diagnostic and clinical tasks, outperformed other systems across 60 clinical centers, and improved management success rate by 10.6% in a prospective study.
AI.conclusion: This development represents a shift in clinical AI from isolated predictions to comprehensive longitudinal management of complex healthcare pathways.

title: Ring-Zero: Scaling Zero RL to a Trillion Parameters for Emergent Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards without human-annotated data, often referred to as zero RL, has emerged as a powerful paradigm for eliciting chain-of-thought reasoning. However, due to computational constraints, existing studies are largely restricted to small models, leaving the training dynamics and emergent capabilities at a large scale unexplored. To meaningfully explore this frontier, we aim to elicit high-quality reasoning behaviors from the model. However, we find that naive scaling often suffers from poor readability, token redundancy, and a lack of adaptive reasoning depth. To address these challenges, we present a stable and efficient training pipeline, incorporating algorithmic and system optimizations such as clipped importance sampling, training-inference ratio correction, and mixed-precision control. Our experiments offer three key findings that validate the "bitter lesson" of scaling: (1) scaling to 1T parameters significantly enhances sample efficiency and performance ceilings; (2) the training process progresses sequentially through an initial discovery phase followed by a sharpening phase; and (3) the model spontaneously develops advanced cognitive behaviors, including anthropomorphism, structured formatting, self-verification, parallel reasoning, and context anxiety, rendering hand-crafted heuristics redundant. Evaluated on seven mathematical benchmarks, Ring-2.5-1T-Zero achieves competitive performance. Additionally, to assess CoT quality beyond final-answer correctness, we propose a structured evaluation framework across three dimensions: comprehensibility, reproducibility, and efficiency, where our model demonstrates clear advantages in producing structured and concise reasoning traces. By sharing our observed emergent phenomena, we hope to provide the community with deeper insights into scaling behaviors, particularly at the 1-trillion scale.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards at a trillion-parameter scale, addressing challenges in scaling and reasoning depth with a novel training pipeline, leading to emergent cognitive behaviors in large models.
AI.motivation: The study aims to advance reasoning in zero-shot reinforcement learning by improving model scalability and understanding its emergent behaviors and training dynamics at larger scales.
AI.method: The authors developed a stable and efficient training pipeline with optimizations like clipped importance sampling, training-inference ratio correction, and mixed-precision control to elicit quality reasoning and overcome challenges in scaling large models.
AI.result: Key findings include improved sample efficiency, sequential training progress phases, and the emergence of advanced cognitive behaviors such as structured reasoning and adaptive depth at the 1-trillion parameter scale.
AI.conclusion: Scaling reinforcement learning models to 1 trillion parameters enhances performance and facilitates advanced reasoning capabilities, providing deeper insights into training dynamics and emergent phenomena at a large scale.

title: Open-Source Intelligence for Code Provenance and the Security Patterns that Separate Human and Large-Language-Model Implementations of Common Programming Tasks
summary: Developers now draw code from two very different sources, the accumulated human answers on sites such as Stack Overflow and the output of large language models. We ask two questions about that split. First, can the provenance of a code snippet be recovered from the code itself, and second, do the two sources differ in the security patterns they adopt for the same task. Using only open sources, a public gateway of open-weight language models and the public Stack Overflow API, we build a fully reproducible pipeline that collects real implementations of 31 security-sensitive programming tasks, among them OAuth with PKCE, JWT verification, password hashing, and SQL access, from 9 language models and from human answers, and scores every sample with deterministic security and style detectors. On 528 real samples we train a cross-validated classifier that recovers human versus model provenance with 93 percent accuracy against a 78 percent baseline, and a 7-way classifier that attributes a sample to the specific model that wrote it at 48 percent. We then report where the sources diverge on security, which patterns models adopt more often than the human corpus and which they inherit from it. Running the same tasks in Python, JavaScript, Go, and Java, we find the security divergence holds in every language while the provenance boundary is partly language-specific and does not transfer symmetrically between them. A vulnerability repair case study, in which models are handed insecure code and asked to fix it, finds a 77 percent repair rate across 21 seeds and 12 weakness classes, but a recurring partial-fix failure in which the model removes the insecure pattern without adding the correct defense. The pipeline is data driven, so any new task or language is added as a single specification entry, and a fail-closed checker re-derives every number in this paper from the stored data.

AI.tldr: The study examines whether the origin of a code snippet (human vs. language model-generated) can be determined and compares their security practices. Using a reproducible pipeline, it achieves high classification accuracy for distinguishing code provenance and identifies differences in security patterns across languages.
AI.motivation: To understand and evaluate the differences between human-written code (e.g., from Stack Overflow) and large language model-generated code in terms of origin identification and security practices, which has implications for software development and security.
AI.method: The authors used a reproducible pipeline to collect 528 code samples across 31 security-sensitive tasks in multiple programming languages, scored the samples using security and style detectors, built classifiers to predict code provenance, and compared security patterns. They also conducted a vulnerability-repair case study.
AI.result: They achieved a 93% accuracy in distinguishing human-written vs. model-generated code. Discrepancies in security approaches were observed across languages, and while models showed a 77% repair rate in handling insecure code, partial-fix failures frequently occurred.
AI.conclusion: The study reveals that code provenance can often be identified, and notable divergences exist in the security patterns of human vs. language model-generated code. However, language models, while capable of repairing security vulnerabilities, often fail to provide comprehensive fixes.

title: What Does a Temporal Benchmark Score Measure? Decomposing Channel Use in Video VLM Evaluation
summary: A score on a temporal video question answering benchmark is meant to measure that a model has temporal understanding, but it conflates two questions. 1. The task question: is the question even temporal, does it need several frames and their order? and 2. The channel question, when it does, does the model recover the order from the pixels, or read it off the positional encoding (RoPE)? Most of a temporal score answers neither, a single frame and answer priors often carry it. The field's validity checks, frame-shuffle sensitivity and the accuracy gained from the full video, speak only to the task question. We contribute a label-free screen for the channel question, the reversal-drop: the accuracy lost when the visual sequence is reversed while RoPE remains forward. It can be applied to compatible temporal benchmarks without new annotations. Paired reverse labels, or tasks whose labels transform deterministically under reversal, distinguish models that follow reversed content from those merely disrupted by the conflict. Molmo2 answers the forward event reading order off positions, while Qwen3-VL answers the reversed event it actually sees, reading visual order (comparatively). We call them position-dominant and visual-sequence-dominant. The split holds across two benchmarks and several temporal tasks at two scales, and activation patching shows it is a real internal property, not an artifact of the conflict. The distinction matters, the two channels fail on opposite inputs so two models with similar score are not interchangable, i.e. an aggregate score does not reflect potential failure modes.

AI.tldr: Temporal video question answering tasks risk conflating whether a model truly understands temporal order from pixels or just utilizes positional encodings. The authors propose a new technique, reversal-drop, to distinguish between these channels without additional annotations.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to ensure that temporal understanding models genuinely capture visual temporal order from video content rather than relying on positional encodings or priors.
AI.method: They introduce the reversal-drop method, which measures performance loss when the visual sequence is reversed, with positional encodings kept constant. Paired reverse labels help identify models' capabilities in interpreting reversed vs. actual sequences.
AI.result: The study distinguishes between two types of models: position-dominant models relying on positional encodings and visual-sequence-dominant models using visual order. This distinction holds consistently across benchmarks and task scales.
AI.conclusion: An aggregate score on temporal benchmarks does not account for models' potential failure modes, highlighting the importance of evaluating models' approaches to temporal understanding.

title: ExToken: Structured Exploration for Efficient Vision-Language-Action Reinforcement Fine-tuning
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has demonstrated significant potential for improving Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models on complex manipulation tasks. However, its practical scalability remains severely limited by the substantial cost of environmental interactions. In this work, we first investigate the exploration stagnation bottleneck in current VLA-RL frameworks and reveal that trajectory diversity is fundamentally more important to sample efficiency than the sheer quantity of collected rollouts. Motivated by these insights, we introduce RL Exploration Token (ExToken), a simple yet general framework that condition VLA policies on discrete behavioral priors derived from offline demonstrations for structured exploration. By conditioning the policy on different tokens during rollout collection, ExToken encourages the agent to explore diverse behavioral modes, substantially improving state-action coverage and exploration efficiency. To bridge exploration during training with deterministic inference at deployment, ExToken further incorporates a state-conditioned token selector that adaptively predicts effective behavioral modes for unseen scenarios. Extensive experiments across simulated and real-world robotic manipulation tasks demonstrate that ExToken consistently accelerates convergence, improves task performance, and exhibits strong robustness under highly constrained interaction budgets.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ExToken, a framework for RL that improves exploration by conditioning policies on diverse behavioral tokens derived from offline data, tackling scalability issues in Vision-Language-Action tasks.
AI.motivation: Address the scalability bottlenecks in RL for complex Vision-Language-Action tasks caused by costly environmental interactions by improving exploration efficiency.
AI.method: Propose ExToken, which uses discrete behavioral priors derived from offline demonstrations to guide exploration. It includes a token selector for state-conditioned behavior prediction.
AI.result: ExToken enhances trajectory diversity, accelerates training convergence, improves task performance, and demonstrates robustness under limited interaction constraints.
AI.conclusion: The approach significantly boosts RL scalability and effectiveness for manipulation tasks by improving structured exploration and enabling better generalization to unseen scenarios.

title: Vertical Standardisation for High-Risk AI Systems under the EU AI Act: A Domain-Specific Framework for Algorithmic Hiring
summary: According to the recent European legislation, high-risk AI systems will have to adapt in order to comply with requirements related to specific areas, like risk management, data quality and governance, logging and traceability, technical documentation, transparency, human oversight, and accuracy, as outlined in the European Artificial Intelligence (AI) Act. As the standardisation process for AI is expected to remain iterative and, so far, there are no European standards on AI fully covering the challenges of algorithmic hiring, we propose specific standardisation-oriented recommendations related to the relevant AI areas specified by the European Commission.
